agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving most implementation details undefined. This is an early-stage product concept communicated informally at an all-hands meeting — not a formal specification.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of current delivery request volume relative to some baseline or capacity — exact definition unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme or unreasonable pricing — thresholds undefined" },
      { "term": "Real-time pricing adjustment", "definition": "The ability to change delivery prices dynamically in response to current demand conditions, without manual intervention" },
      { "term": "Fully automated", "definition": "The system adjusts prices without human approval per adjustment — implies no manual step in the pricing loop" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery price must increase when demand is high relative to a defined baseline or capacity",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business pricing policy. A VP could decide to freeze prices during a PR crisis or promotional period. The direction of price movement is a strategy choice, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand exceeds the 'high' threshold, when a price is calculated, then the resulting price is greater than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery price must decrease when demand is low relative to a defined baseline or capacity",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Same as BR-1 — this is a competitive pricing strategy, not a system integrity constraint. Management could choose to hold prices flat during low demand instead.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand is below the 'low' threshold, when a price is calculated, then the resulting price is less than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — the system must use current demand data, not stale or batch-processed data",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The definition of 'real time' is a policy/SLA decision (sub-second? within 5 minutes?). A VP could relax this to near-real-time. However, using grossly stale data would violate the product's core value proposition.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand conditions change, when a new delivery is priced, then the price reflects demand data no older than [undefined SLA threshold]"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The pricing system must operate without manual human intervention for individual price adjustments (fully automated)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is a design goal, not a physical constraint. The business could introduce manual override or approval steps at any time. This is an operational policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a delivery is requested, when the system calculates its price, then no human approval step is required before the price is presented to the customer"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from exceeding acceptable bounds (both upper and lower)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The existence of guardrails is a policy decision. The specific thresholds are entirely undefined. However, note: a price floor of > 0 (BR-6) is arguably an invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given any demand level, when a price is calculated, then the resulting price falls within the configured minimum and maximum bounds"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A delivery price must always be a positive monetary amount greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically and financially nonsensical — the company would be paying customers to deliver. This is a hard constraint rooted in the nature of a commercial transaction, not a policy choice. If this is violated, the system is producing corrupt output.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given any demand level and any guardrail configuration, when a price is calculated, then the price is strictly greater than zero"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing function must be deterministic for the same inputs: given identical demand level, route, and configuration, the same price must be produced",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If the same inputs yield different prices, the system has hidden state corruption or race conditions. Determinism for identical inputs is a data integrity requirement — customers seeing different prices for the same conditions at the same instant would indicate system corruption.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given the same demand snapshot, route, and pricing configuration, when price is calculated twice, then both results are identical"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must be correlated to demand — the relationship between demand and price must be monotonically non-decreasing (higher demand never produces a lower price than lower demand, all else equal)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If higher demand produces a lower price than lower demand (with all other inputs equal), the pricing model is mathematically corrupt. This is not a policy — it is an internal consistency constraint that, if violated, means the algorithm is broken.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given two demand levels D1 > D2 with all other inputs equal, when prices P1 and P2 are calculated, then P1 >= P2"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "The system must maintain profitability — the dynamically adjusted price should not fall below the cost of fulfilling the delivery",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The CEO explicitly states the goal of being 'profitable.' However, businesses routinely operate at a loss on individual transactions for strategic reasons (market entry, retention). A VP could authorize below-cost pricing. This is a strong business preference, not a hard invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a delivery with known fulfillment cost C, when a price P is calculated, then P >= C (under default guardrail configuration)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "The system must maintain competitiveness — pricing should not be so high during peak demand that it drives customers to competitors",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Competitiveness is a strategic goal, not a system constraint. The specific upper bound is entirely undefined and would be a business policy decision tied to market analysis.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given high demand, when a price is calculated, then the price does not exceed the configured maximum guardrail ceiling"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand data is temporarily unavailable (feed outage, latency spike)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Should the system fall back to a base price? Refuse to quote? Use last-known demand? This is a critical gap." },
    { "scenario": "Demand oscillates rapidly around a threshold boundary", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Prices could flicker between high and low tiers within seconds, confusing customers. Needs hysteresis or smoothing policy." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail ceiling is set below fulfillment cost", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Conflicting constraints — the system cannot be both profitable and below the ceiling. Configuration validation is needed." },
    { "scenario": "Two customers request the same route at the same instant but see different prices due to a demand threshold crossing between requests", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. This is a fairness and potential legal/regulatory concern. Needs a price-lock window or consistency policy." },
    { "scenario": "Extreme demand event (e.g., natural disaster, major event)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Price surge during emergencies may violate price-gouging regulations in some jurisdictions. Guardrails alone may be insufficient — may need event-based overrides." },
    { "scenario": "Demand is zero (no requests in the measurement window)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Price should presumably be at or near the floor, but what does the model output when demand is literally zero?" },
    { "scenario": "A customer is quoted a price, then demand shifts before they confirm — which price applies?", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Needs a price-lock/quote-validity window. This is a fundamental UX and business fairness question." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue and margin on every delivery. Upside: capture surplus during high demand, stimulate volume during low demand. Downside: miscalibrated guardrails or algorithm errors could cause revenue leakage (floor too low) or demand destruction (ceiling too high). Below-cost pricing during low demand periods is a real risk if guardrails are not anchored to fulfillment cost.",
    "operational": "Fully automated pricing removes manual pricing decisions but introduces new operational complexity: demand signal infrastructure, real-time computation, guardrail configuration management, monitoring for anomalies, and incident response when the algorithm misbehaves. The ops team needs observability into why prices are what they are.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing — this is a significant UX shift. Transparency is critical: customers must understand WHY a price is what it is, or trust erodes. Unpredictable pricing frustrates repeat customers who expect consistency. Price-lock windows and clear communication are essential for user satisfaction.",
    "risk": "HIGH. (1) Regulatory risk: price-gouging laws in many jurisdictions during emergencies. (2) Reputational risk: perception of exploitative pricing during high-demand events. (3) Technical risk: algorithm errors at scale affect every transaction simultaneously. (4) Competitive risk: if guardrails are miscalibrated, prices may consistently exceed market rates. (5) Legal risk: differential pricing may trigger discrimination concerns in some markets.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "See variable prices — may pay more during peak, less during off-peak. Pricing predictability decreases. Trust depends on transparency." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Demand-based pricing likely affects their compensation or assignment volume. High-demand surcharges may or may not flow to them — this is unaddressed." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Revenue forecasting becomes more complex with dynamic pricing. Need new models and reporting." },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Must maintain demand signal infrastructure and pricing system SLA. New monitoring and alerting requirements." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability. Need policies for price disputes and refund scenarios." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review for price-gouging regulations, discrimination concerns, and consumer protection requirements per jurisdiction." },
      { "group": "Product/Marketing", "impact": "Must communicate pricing model to customers. Transparency and positioning are critical to avoid backlash." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 25,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement from an all-hands meeting, not a product specification. Almost every critical detail is undefined: demand measurement methodology, guardrail thresholds, base price calculation, price-lock windows, update frequency SLA, cost floor anchoring, quote validity, fairness policies, and regulatory compliance approach. The 10 rules extracted are largely inferred from first principles and domain knowledge — not explicitly stated. Only 3 things are actually stated: prices go up with high demand, down with low demand, and there will be 'some guardrails.' Everything else is inference. Acceptance criteria cannot be responsibly defined from this input.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Price must be positive (BR-6) — this is a universal commercial invariant",
      "Price correlates positively with demand (BR-1, BR-2) — explicitly stated by CEO",
      "System is automated (BR-4) — explicitly stated by CEO",
      "Guardrails exist (BR-5) — explicitly stated by CEO"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand — no thresholds defined",
      "What the guardrail bounds are — CEO said 'probably' and 'so prices don't go crazy'",
      "Whether profitability is enforced per-transaction or in aggregate",
      "How 'real-time' is defined — sub-second? per-minute? per-hour?",
      "Whether courier compensation is affected by dynamic pricing",
      "How price quotes are locked or expire",
      "Regulatory compliance strategy for price-gouging laws",
      "Whether there is a base/reference price and how it's determined"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A base price exists from which adjustments are made (not stated, but implied by 'adjust')",
      "Demand is measurable in a meaningful, quantifiable way",
      "Guardrails include both an upper ceiling and a lower floor",
      "The system applies to delivery pricing specifically, not to product pricing",
      "Price changes are visible to customers before they commit to a delivery"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The definition of 'demand' is completely undefined. Is it request volume per time window? Per zone? Per route? Relative to courier capacity? The entire pricing model depends on this definition and it is not specified.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product", "Engineering", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Define demand as request count per zone per time window", "Define demand as ratio of requests to available couriers", "Define demand as percentile rank against historical baseline"],
      "recommendation": "Define demand as the ratio of active delivery requests to available courier capacity per geographic zone — this captures both supply and demand sides and is the most meaningful signal for pricing."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrail thresholds are entirely undefined. 'So prices don't go crazy' is not a specification. Without specific bounds, the system cannot be implemented or tested.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Legal/Compliance", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Fixed dollar min/max per delivery", "Percentage-based cap relative to base price (e.g., 0.5x to 3x)", "Cost-anchored floor with market-anchored ceiling"],
      "recommendation": "Use a multiplier-based guardrail system (e.g., 0.7x to 2.5x base price) with a hard floor at fulfillment cost. Specific values need Finance and Legal input."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Quote validity / price-lock policy is not mentioned. If a customer sees a price and demand changes before confirmation, which price applies? This is fundamental to the customer experience and has legal implications.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["Price locked at quote time for N minutes", "Price locked at confirmation time only", "Price guaranteed if confirmed within a session"],
      "recommendation": "Lock the quoted price for a defined window (e.g., 5 minutes). This is standard practice in ride-hailing and delivery platforms."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory compliance. Dynamic pricing is subject to price-gouging laws in many jurisdictions, especially during declared emergencies. This is not optional — it is a legal requirement that could result in fines or injunctions.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Finance", "Executive Team"],
      "options": ["Add jurisdiction-aware pricing caps during declared emergencies", "Disable dynamic pricing entirely during emergencies", "Cap surge multiplier at a legally safe threshold globally"],
      "recommendation": "Legal review is mandatory before launch. At minimum, implement emergency-mode overrides that cap or disable surge pricing during declared emergencies per jurisdiction."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The relationship between dynamic delivery pricing and courier compensation is unaddressed. If customers pay more during surges, do couriers earn more? This affects courier supply, fairness, and potentially labor regulations.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Couriers/Drivers", "Operations", "Legal/Compliance", "HR"],
      "options": ["Pass through a percentage of surge to courier pay", "Keep courier pay fixed regardless of dynamic pricing", "Offer surge bonuses to couriers independently"],
      "recommendation": "Pass a meaningful portion of surge pricing to couriers — this incentivizes supply during high-demand periods and is the economically rational behavior. Without this, surge pricing increases revenue but doesn't solve the underlying supply constraint."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
